title: Fancy Dress-Up Tower
summary: A kids' dress-up wardrobe from half a sheet of plywood: shaped sides, a hanging rod for costumes, and shelves. Endlessly customizable with hooks and a mirror.
description:
This is a child's dress-up station — a tall, open wardrobe with a hanging rod for costumes and cloaks, plus shelves for shoes, hats, and props. It comes out of half a sheet of 3/4" plywood plus a bit of 1x4, and the whole personality comes from the shaped sides you cut yourself and the extras you add: hooks, a mirror, moulding.

The efficient start is to have the store rip your plywood into two 16"-wide strips and one 15"-wide strip — it saves ripping at home and makes the sheet easy to transport. Then crosscut the shelves and trim from those strips.

The shaped sides are the one part demanding care. You need two identical curved sides, so draw the pattern on one, practice on scraps with your jigsaw until you're confident, cut it, and use it as a template for the second. Mind the kerf — be deliberate about which side of the line you cut on. Sand thoroughly; this project wants a painted finish (or iron-on edge banding if you'd rather show the plywood edge). Assembly is pocket holes or brad nails hidden under the shelves. The hanging rod uses closet rod-pocket holders and a 1-1/4" dowel sized to fit them. From there it's yours to fancy up — hooks for necklaces and capes, stick-on mirror tiles, glued-on moulding, a French pinboard. That's what turns half a sheet of plywood into a gift a kid loves.

tools.note: The shaped curved sides — the signature detail.
Sand the cut curves thoroughly before paint.
For hidden joints; brads or 2" screws also work.

materials.name: Plywood, 3/4 in, half sheet
materials.note: Sides and shelves. Have the store rip it into 16" and 15" strips.

materials.name: Wood dowel, 1-1/4 in, 16 in
materials.note: Hanging rod — match diameter to the rod holders.

materials.name: Pocket-hole screws, 1-1/4 in / brad nails / 2 in screws

cutList.note: Cut the shaped profile — template the pair to match.

steps.title: Break down the plywood
steps.body: If the store can rip your plywood into two 16"-wide strips and one 15"-wide strip, it saves time and makes transport easy. Otherwise rip the plywood with the grain into strips per the diagram, then crosscut the shelves and trim boards.

steps.title: Cut the two shaped sides
steps.body: Mark the pattern on a large side piece. You need two identical sides, so practice on scraps with your jigsaw until you're confident, cut one, and use it as a template for the other. Mind the kerf — be aware which side of the line you cut on. Sand, and sand some more. This project works best painted (or iron on edge banding if you want to show the plywood edge).

steps.title: Cut the bottom and top trim
steps.body: Cut the two bottom trim pieces with the same care as the sides, then cut the top trim from the 1x4 pieces.

steps.title: Attach sides to shelves
steps.body: Mark the insides of the bottom trim 2" from the top. Attach the sides to the shelves, hiding pocket holes (if used) on the undersides of the shelves.
materials: Pocket-hole screws, 1-1/4 in / brad nails / 2 in screws

steps.title: Assemble the tower
steps.body: Attach the sides to the base, then attach the top shelf to the sides, squaring the cabinet as you go.
materials: Pocket-hole screws, 1-1/4 in / brad nails / 2 in screws

steps.title: Add the rod and customize
steps.body: Add the rod-pocket holders with their included screws, then cut and place the wood dowel in the rod pockets as the hanging bar. For extra flair, glue wood moulding to the sides. Then get creative: hooks for necklaces, hats, and capes, a stick-on mirror, or a French pinboard. The details are what turn half a sheet of plywood into a real gift. Paint to finish.

images.alt: Kids' dress-up wardrobe tower with a hanging rod and shelves
